structure.title: Preface
structure.summary: This preface section provides an overview of the document "Requirements on Operating System Interface" and introduces key aspects such as its ownership by AUTOSAR, its status, and its integration within the broader AUTOSAR standard. It outlines the document's scope, conventions for use, and change history across various releases. The summary also highlights important relationships between this document and other parts of the AUTOSAR standard, particularly the Execution Management section, and mentions the protection of intellectual property rights associated with the material. Technical details include updates to C++17 standards and changes in requirements over different release cycles.

structure.title: 1 Scope of Document
structure.summary: This section outlines the scope of the document, which focuses on defining the requirements for the Operating System (OS) component within the Foundation of the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform. Key concepts include the specific needs and specifications that the OS must meet to be compatible with the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform. The document establishes a clear relationship between the OS and other components of the platform, ensuring seamless integration and functionality. While no tables are explicitly mentioned in this excerpt, it sets the stage for detailed technical requirements and constraints that will be elaborated upon in subsequent sections.

structure.title: 2 Conventions to be used
structure.summary: This section of the "Requirements on Operating System Interface" document outlines specific conventions to be used when interfacing with an operating system. It introduces key terms such as "interface layer," which refers to the software components responsible for communication between applications and the OS, and "API standards," defining the set of rules and protocols that must be adhered to for consistent interaction. The section emphasizes the importance of these conventions in ensuring compatibility and reliability across different systems. While no tables are explicitly mentioned, it implies that adherence to these conventions is crucial for developers to follow when designing or modifying software interfaces with operating systems.

nodes.title: 2.1 Document Conventions
nodes.summary: This section of the "Requirements on Operating System Interface" document outlines the conventions for representing and expressing requirements within AUTOSAR documents. It specifies that these representations must adhere to a particular table format defined in [TPS_STDT_00078], as detailed in the Standardization Template referenced [1]. Additionally, it mandates the use of specific verbal forms for indicating obligations, as outlined in [TPS_STDT_00053], also found within the Standardization Template. These conventions ensure consistency and clarity in requirement documentation, facilitating effective communication among stakeholders involved in AUTOSAR projects.

nodes.title: 2.2 Requirements Guidelines
nodes.summary: The section "2.2 Requirements Guidelines" in the document "Requirements on Operating System Interface" outlines the key requirements and guidelines for interfacing with an operating system. It introduces essential concepts such as interface standards, compatibility levels, and performance benchmarks. The guidelines emphasize the importance of adhering to these specifications to ensure seamless integration and optimal functionality. While no specific tables are mentioned, the section likely includes detailed criteria for testing and validation processes that must be followed to meet the outlined requirements.

nodes.title: 2.2.1 Requirements status
nodes.summary: This section outlines the status of certain requirements related to the operating system interface as detailed in the document. It specifically mentions that requirements [RS_OSI_00204], [RS_OSI_00208], and [RS_OSI_00209] are currently not included in the current release but may be considered for future updates. The section indicates that these functionalities are subject to modification, suggesting an ongoing development process where changes could be made before their eventual inclusion. No technical details from tables are explicitly mentioned in this excerpt, focusing instead on the status and potential future integration of these requirements.

structure.title: 3 Acronyms and abbreviations
structure.summary: This section of the "Requirements on Operating System Interface" document provides a glossary of key acronyms and abbreviations relevant to operating system interfaces in an AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform context. It includes definitions for terms such as Operating System, Operating System Interface (OSI), Execution Management, Adaptive Application, ARTI, and AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform, among others. The relationships between these components are highlighted, particularly how the OSI is a Functional Cluster within the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform that interfaces with both adaptive applications and the platform itself. Additionally, it references external documents for detailed definitions of some terms like Execution Management, Task (or pthread in POSIX), and others, emphasizing their interdependencies and roles within the system architecture.

structure.title: 4 Requirements Specification
structure.summary: This section of the "Requirements on Operating System Interface" document outlines all the necessary requirements for selecting an operating system suitable for the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform. It introduces key concepts such as compatibility and performance criteria that must be met by potential OS candidates. The chapter also describes important relationships between these requirements, emphasizing how they interconnect to ensure seamless integration with the platform. While no specific tables are mentioned in this excerpt, it sets the stage for detailed technical specifications that would follow, guiding the selection process based on stringent criteria.

nodes.title: 4.1 Functional Overview
nodes.summary: This section of the "Requirements on Operating System Interface" document provides an overview of the operating system's role in managing runtime resources for applications and functional clusters within the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform. Key concepts include the operating system’s cooperation with Execution Management for platform initialization and application start-up/shutdown, as well as its provision of essential mechanisms that are difficult or inefficient to implement by other software entities. The document distinguishes between requirements directly supporting adaptive applications and those necessary for implementing functional clusters, particularly highlighting the critical role of these mechanisms in the Execution Management process.

nodes.title: 4.2 Functional Requirements
nodes.summary: The section "4.2 Functional Requirements" in the document "Requirements on Operating System Interface" outlines the specific functionalities that an operating system must support to meet the project's needs. It introduces key concepts such as interface protocols, service APIs, and user interaction mechanisms. The summary highlights important relationships between these components and any constraints or processes related to their implementation. While no tables are explicitly mentioned in this excerpt, it is noted that detailed technical specifications for each functional requirement are provided elsewhere in the document, including specific parameters and performance metrics for different operating system interfaces.

nodes.title: 4.2.1 Assumption of Use
nodes.summary: This section discusses the execution environment for Adaptive Applications within the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform, which are not formal requirements but assumptions that guide the development of these applications. Key concepts include the properties and behaviors expected of Processes running on this platform. The Operating System section outlines specific requirements that processes must meet to function correctly, establishing a foundation for deriving relevant technical specifications from use cases. While no tables are explicitly mentioned, the document emphasizes the importance of aligning application assumptions with the platform's capabilities to ensure proper functionality.

nodes.title: 4.2.2 General Requirements
nodes.summary: This section of the "Requirements on Operating System Interface" document outlines key requirements for the operating system interface in the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform. It introduces concepts such as POSIX PSE51 compliance and support for C++, emphasizing that these are essential for ensuring application portability and compatibility with specific programming languages. The section also details how the operating system should enable processes to react to external stimuli from devices, start execution management, and integrate various functionalities like synchronization primitives and timers. These requirements ensure a robust and adaptable environment for adaptive applications.

nodes.title: 4.2.3 Operating System Requirements
nodes.summary: This section of the "Requirements on Operating System Interface" document outlines key requirements for an operating system to support robust and secure multi-process environments in a software-defined context. It introduces concepts such as memory budgeting, CPU time allocation, process binding to specific cores, access control mechanisms, application isolation, shared code and data usage, restricted functionality access, error handling, and observability of scheduling information. These requirements are interrelated, with constraints and dependencies often aligning around ensuring system stability, security, and efficient resource utilization. The section also highlights the importance of providing detailed trace and scheduling information to applications for diagnostic purposes, reflecting a balance between operational efficiency and system transparency.

nodes.title: 4.3 Non-Functional Requirements
nodes.summary: The section "4.3 Non-Functional Requirements" in the document "Requirements on Operating System Interface" appears to be empty, indicating that there are currently no specific non-functional requirements outlined for the operating system interface. Non-functional requirements typically encompass aspects such as performance, security, usability, and reliability of the system. In a typical section covering these requirements, one might expect to find definitions or descriptions of key concepts like response time, throughput, and fault tolerance, along with any constraints or processes related to meeting these criteria. However, since this section is currently devoid of content, it suggests that either no non-functional requirements have been identified yet, or they are being handled elsewhere in the document.

structure.title: 5 References
structure.summary: This section of the "Requirements on Operating System Interface" document outlines the references and traceable changes in requirements across different AUTOSAR releases. It introduces key concepts such as POSIX PSE51 compliance, support for C++, and mechanisms for memory and CPU time budgeting. The document details how these requirements have evolved from R23-11 to R25-11, highlighting added, changed, and deleted items in each release. Importantly, it also mentions the support for multi-process isolation and error handling, with specific tables (A.1-A.5) listing detailed changes across different versions.
